serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately 550 g, representing a full bibimbap bowl combo with simmered beef, rice, vegetables, kimchi, gochujang, fried garlic, chili garlic flakes, and a half-boiled egg as served at Sukiya Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Bibimbap (mixed rice with meat and vegetables) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; supplemented with USDA entries for simmered beef, cooked short-grain rice, kimchi, spinach, bean sprouts, egg, and gochujang
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for this specific Sukiya menu item. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composite using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked Japanese short-grain rice (FDC 168878), simmered/braised beef (FDC 174032), kimchi (FDC 169999), cooked spinach (FDC 170091), cooked mung bean sprouts (FDC 169957), hard/soft-boiled egg (FDC 173424), gochujang (FDC 1103494), and fried garlic (FDC 169230). Estimated serving weight 550 g; per-100g values derived from composite. Sodium elevated due to kimchi, gochujang, and simmered beef seasoning.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed rice dish with meat and vegetables (bibimbap analogue, GI ~54)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific Sukiya bibimbap bowl. GI of 54 is estimated using the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked Japanese short-grain rice (GI ~54–56, low-medium) as the dominant carbohydrate source (~60% of carbohydrate mass). The presence of protein (beef, egg), fat, dietary fibre (vegetables, kimchi), and acidity (kimchi, gochujang) is expected to moderate the overall GI toward the lower-medium range. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (54 × 108.9) / 100 ≈ 58.9, where available carbohydrate per serving = 19.8 g/100g × 550 g / 100 × 100 = 108.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100g; ~2310 mg per serving) from kimchi, gochujang, and seasoned simmered beef
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium load (~2310 mg per serving, approaching the daily recommended limit of 2000 mg), and should request reduced-sodium modifications or consume only a partial portion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~54) and high glycemic load per serving (~58.9 g) driven by a large portion of Japanese short-grain rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with this dish due to its high glycemic load per full serving; portion control is essential, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables or reducing the rice portion can help blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from simmered beef, partially offset by beneficial vegetables, kimchi probiotics, and garlic
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content is the primary concern; the dish contains moderate saturated fat (~1.4 g/100g) and beneficial components such as garlic, spinach, and bean sprouts, but the overall sodium load warrants portion restriction and dietary counselling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Fried Garlic and Chili Garlic Flakes Beef Bibimbap Bowl Combo is a Japanese-Korean fusion rice bowl dish served at Sukiya, a Japanese gyudon chain operating in Singapore. The bowl features Sukiya's signature simmered beef (gyudon-style, slow-cooked in a sweet-savoury dashi-soy broth), placed over 100% Japan-harvested short-grain rice. It is topped with whole fried garlic pieces and chili garlic flakes for aromatic depth and heat, seasoned bean sprouts and blanched spinach for vegetable balance, kimchi (fermented spicy cabbage) for probiotic tang, gochujang (Korean sweet and spicy red pepper paste) for umami and heat, and a half-boiled egg for richness and protein. The dish is served as a combo, typically including a side item. Nutritionally, it is a calorie-dense, protein-moderate, high-carbohydrate meal with significant sodium from the kimchi, gochujang, and seasoned beef. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 54 (low), driven primarily by the Japanese short-grain rice, though the protein, fat, fibre, and acidity from kimchi and gochujang moderate the glycemic response. The dish is not suitable for low-sodium diets and requires portion control for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. Garlic provides allicin-related cardiovascular benefits, and kimchi contributes beneficial lactic acid bacteria. The dish is not vegetarian, not vegan, and not certified halal.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN) for this specific Sukiya menu item. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-ingredient composite using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for the identified components. GI is analogously estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using Japanese short-grain rice as the dominant carbohydrate source. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient list is well-defined from the menu description and the USDA component data is high quality.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries; no single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. 2. Serving weight of 550 g is an estimate for a full combo bowl; actual weight may vary by restaurant preparation. 3. Sodium estimate (~420 mg/100g) is conservative; actual sodium may be higher depending on the quantity of kimchi, gochujang, and soy-based beef seasoning used. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this composite. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this composite. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component entries used for this composite. 7. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese short-grain rice as the dominant glycaemic carbohydrate; the actual GI of this composite dish has not been directly measured. 8. Halal status is not confirmed; Sukiya Singapore uses beef but halal certification status should be verified with the restaurant. 9. The cuisine tag 'Japanese Curry' and 'Grilled Chicken Skewers' in the input were not relevant to this dish and have been removed. 10. The dish is classified as Japanese cuisine (Sukiya is a Japanese chain) with Korean-inspired toppings (bibimbap, kimchi, gochujang); the mandatory Japanese-language name has been provided per G6 multilingual rules.
